primary_diagnosis.name: Diabetic ketoacidosis precipitated by intercurrent illness and omitted insulin in known type 1 diabetes

pathophysiology_summary: Absolute insulin deficiency (omitted insulin doses during an intercurrent viral illness, when counter-regulatory stress hormones are already raised) drives unchecked lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis. Accumulating ketoacids (beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate) produce a high anion-gap metabolic acidosis; the deep, rapid Kussmaul respiratory pattern is respiratory compensation and the fruity acetone breath is exhaled acetone. Marked hyperglycaemia causes an osmotic diuresis with profound polyuria, polydipsia and litres of free-water and electrolyte loss, giving the sunken eyes, poor skin turgor, tachycardia and hypotension of severe hypovolaemia. Total-body potassium is depleted even when serum potassium is normal or high, which is why insulin must be given only with fluid resuscitation and potassium monitoring in hospital — a large insulin dose without potassium cover can precipitate fatal hypokalaemia. Untreated, the trajectory is hypovolaemic shock, cerebral oedema, coma and death.

differential.evidence_basis: Known type 1 diabetic, vomiting and weakness with an off-the-scale home glucose reading — DKA until proven otherwise.
Vomiting and diffuse abdominal cramps could be a viral gastro — but do not explain the polyuria, deep breathing or extreme hyperglycaemia.
Abdominal pain warrants consideration, though the pain is diffuse without peritonism and is preceded by polyuria/polydipsia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, DKA must already be the leading concern in a type 1 diabetic with vomiting and a 'HI' meter reading; anchoring on gastroenteritis here is the premature-closure trap.

differential.evidence_basis: Polyuria/polydipsia + omitted insulin + intercurrent illness + deep rapid breathing + sweet breath + off-scale glucose is the classic precipitant-and-presentation constellation.
Also profoundly hyperglycaemic and dehydrated, but HHS is a type 2 / older-adult picture with minimal ketosis and no Kussmaul breathing — less likely in a young type 1 diabetic.
Cannot account for the deep compensatory breathing, ketotic breath or extreme hyperglycaemia.
Also causes vomiting and a high anion-gap acidosis with tachypnoea, but there is no aspirin ingestion history and the glucose is very high rather than low/normal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once polyuria, Kussmaul breathing and the omitted-insulin history are elicited, DKA is confirmed as the working diagnosis and gastroenteritis is excluded.

differential.diagnosis: diabetic ketoacidosis (severe, with impending hypovolaemic shock)
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed metabolic emergency; hospital blood/gas confirmation and IV fluid + insulin therapy are time-critical.

differential_progression.scoring_note: This is a telehealth-presumptive diagnosis — laboratory confirmation (glucose, ketones, venous gas, electrolytes) happens in hospital; the AI Doctor's job is recognition and immediate escalation, not remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: Deep, rapid (Kussmaul) breathing — respiratory compensation for a severe metabolic acidosis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals severe acidosis; a decompensating patient who tires will lose this compensation and deteriorate rapidly.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise severity and delayed escalation while acidosis worsens toward coma.

red_flags.description: Progressive drowsiness / lethargy — reduced conscious level in a severe metabolic derangement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Declining consciousness threatens airway protection (aspiration risk with vomiting) and heralds cerebral oedema/coma.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unprotected airway, aspiration, or unrecognised progression to coma.

red_flags.description: Off-the-scale home glucose ('HI') with omitted insulin during intercurrent illness
red_flags.clinical_significance: The metabolic driver of DKA; identifies the treatable precipitant and the need for hospital insulin/fluid therapy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The diabetic emergency is mistaken for a benign gastrointestinal illness and under-triaged.

red_flags.description: Signs of severe dehydration / impending hypovolaemic shock (relentless vomiting, litres of fluid loss, sunken eyes, weakness)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Circulatory collapse is imminent without IV rehydration; oral fluids cannot keep pace and are being vomited.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the vomiting and diffuse abdominal pain as a viral gastro and offering oral rehydration/reassurance, missing DKA in a type 1 diabetic.
Attributing the diffuse abdominal pain to a surgical cause; DKA commonly causes abdominal pain that resolves with metabolic correction.

Advising a large 'catch-up' subcutaneous insulin dose at home to bring the sugar down — without fluids and potassium monitoring this risks fatal hypokalaemia; insulin belongs with hospital fluid resuscitation.
Attempting to coach sick-day management at home when the patient is already vomiting, drowsy and hypovolaemic — this is an emergency that needs an ambulance, not remote titration.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Actual blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate
Capillary refill time and skin turgor on examination
Auscultation and objective assessment of the respiratory pattern as Kussmaul breathing
Venous/arterial blood gas, anion gap and serum electrolytes (especially potassium)
A precise blood glucose value beyond the meter's 'HI' ceiling
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance for emergency transport; keep her lying flat with legs elevated, monitor consciousness and breathing, do NOT give a large insulin dose or force oral fluids; hospital delivers IV fluid resuscitation, controlled insulin infusion and potassium replacement.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the category of emergency (a known type 1 diabetic this unwell needs hospital regardless of the exact label); the real risk is UNDER-action — a benign gastroenteritis read that delays the ambulance, or an unsafe home insulin instruction.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ADEA/ADS Australian sick-day management guidelines for type 1 diabetes
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Never omit basal insulin during illness; check ketones; seek urgent medical care for persistent vomiting, drowsiness, deep breathing or ketones that will not clear.
If the patient becomes unresponsive and is not breathing normally, commence CPR and continue until help arrives.
